description: 78M on warfarin, fall with brief LOC — Canadian CT Head excludes anticoagulated patients
transcript:
Dr. Carter: What happened today?
Patient: I tripped on the rug going to the bathroom. Hit my head on the wall.
Dr. Carter: Did you pass out?
Wife: Maybe ten seconds. I was right there. He came around quickly but seemed dazed for a minute.
Dr. Carter: Any vomiting?
Patient: No.
Dr. Carter: Headache?
Patient: A little, on the right side where I hit.
Dr. Carter: Are you on any blood thinners?
Patient: Yes, warfarin. For my atrial fibrillation.
Dr. Carter: When was your last INR?
Patient: Last week, it was 2.4.
Dr. Carter: Any other medical problems?
Patient: A-fib, high blood pressure, mild kidney disease.
Nurse: GCS 15, alert and oriented. HR 72 irregular, BP 142/84, sat 97.
Dr. Carter: Pupils equal, no focal deficits. Tender area right temporal scalp, no laceration, no obvious depression. No basal skull signs.
Dr. Carter: He's anticoagulated and had LOC from a fall. The rule we'd use for non-anticoagulated head injury doesn't really apply here — anticoagulation alone is an indication for imaging in this setting. He's getting a CT regardless. Let me get that ordered, send a STAT INR, and have reversal agents ready in case there's a bleed.

groundTruth.notes: Canadian CT Head explicitly excludes patients on oral anticoagulants — they need imaging on a separate pathway. Doctor verbally identifies this. Surfacing the rule here would push a tool that the doctor has already correctly excluded. This tests whether the engine respects the rule's own exclusion criteria, not just the trigger criteria.
